title: 🎙️ Cariboo Local Affairs
description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Christmas in July Food Drive supports Williams Lake Salvation Army
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

## Christmas in July Food Drive supports Williams Lake Salvation Army Published 6:38 pm Wednesday, July 22, 2026 **By rebeccadyok** The Williams Lake Salvation Army received donations, including a $1,036 cheque, from the Williams Lake Fire Department's annual Christmas in July Food Drive on Wednesday, July 22\. (Photo submitted) In just a few hours, dozens of non-perishable food items and $1,036 in community donations were collected by the Williams Lake Fire Department to support the Salvation Army. Approximately 35 firefighters canvassed neighbourhoods on Tuesday, July 21, from 6 to 9 p.m.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists revive a powerful antibiotic that superbugs had defeated
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.
items.summary: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The
items._excerpt: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. The combination restored the antibiotic’s ability to kill drug-resistant E. faecium, raising hopes that similar chemical helpers could rescue other failing medicines.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Fuel Tank Orca Mural in Palmer Station, Antarctica
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

The fuel tank mural at Palmer Station is one of the most recognizable landmarks at this remote Antarctic research base. Painted on the station’s large fuel tank, the mural features a leaping orca and adds a unique piece of public art to the otherwise industrial scenery. Visible from the water as ships approach the station, it has become a familiar symbol of Palmer Station for residents and visitors alike.

The mural stands out against the surrounding Antarctic landscape and serves as a reminder that even in one of the world’s most isolated workplaces, people bring creativity and personality to their surroundings.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Electrical Box Theatre in Los Angeles, California
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

On the corner of Traction Avenue and 4th Place in Los Angeles’s Arts District sits a gray utility box. Big deal, right?

Actually, yes.

Measuring roughly six feet tall and three feet deep, the city street cabinet is not that at all, even in the slightest—it’s the creation of Downtown LA artist S.C. Mero, and it’s (not literally, but still) electric.

Welcome to the home of the Electrical Box Theatre, possibly the smallest performance space in all of Los Angeles, if not the world. No tickets, seats, nor guest lists. The show starts when the door opens.

By day, it’s a forgotten piece of urban nothing; at night, with its door open to a typically unsuspecting audience, the box becomes a hub for artists and performers, and all the street’s a stage.

“Not to be corny,” Mero says, “but you just don’t know what’s in something unless you look inside it. A person, or even ourselves. I think there’s kind of a beautiful message in that.”

Inside, the theater’s lined with velvet wallpaper and theatrical details, including a clock and photos of the theater itself (even a fake utility box in Los Angeles needs headshots).

Mero is known for public art that transforms overlooked parts of the city into moments of surprise. Some of it’s impossible to miss (like a 13-foot tall mailbox); others, like the Electrical Box Theatre, reward curiosity.

Since opening, it has hosted neighborhood musicians, a clown, a mime, burlesque performances, and even a vampire with a banjo. Performers coordinate with Mero in advance and receive access instructions on the day of their show.

At its street-smart historic corner, the theater also takes a bow to the Arts District’s storied lineage of art and artists, who have long called this block home before slowly getting pushed out by the churn of city real estate reality.

And that’s part of the magic: how temporary it all feels, and the discovery that lives therein. Claiming a piece of the city back for the people, Mero has devoted this patch of sidewalk to the artists who make all things beautiful last.
items.summary: On the corner of Traction Avenue and 4th Place in Los Angeles’s Arts District sits a gray utility box. Big deal, right? Actually, yes. Measuring roughly six feet tall and three feet deep, the city street cabinet is not that at all, even in the slightest—it’s the creation of Downtown LA artist S.C.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

items.title: [New Atlas] Threat of extreme solar storms may be far worse than we imagined
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

A theorized limit to our planet’s response to geomagnetic storms may be the product of a statistical illusion, researchers claim, revealing that the extent of damage from the most extreme solar events could be greater than we ever imagined.

Continue Reading

Category: Astronomy, Science, Refractor: Science & Health

Tags: Sun, Magnetic field, geomagnetic, NASA, coronal mass ejection, solar-storm

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Watching too much TV in midlife may shrink the brain
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Watching television very often in midlife may be linked to troubling brain changes decades later. In a study of about 1,700 adults, frequent TV viewers later showed smaller brain regions involved in memory, decision-making, and visual processing, along with more signs of damage in the brain’s white matter. Surprisingly, sitting itself did not appear to be the main issue, since people who spent long hours seated at mentally engaging jobs often showed healthier brain patterns.
items.summary: Watching television very often in midlife may be linked to troubling brain changes decades later. items.title: [Boing Boing] Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

I just discovered, through Frank Santopadre's podcast Fun for All Ages, the radio and television universe of artist Rachel Lichtman's imagined 1960s/1970s town of Golden Sands.

It's a surreal town stuck in a mid-century far out brown-and-orange aesthetic. Lichtman creates parody television commercials, and print and radio ads from the town of Golden Sands, as well as opening credit theme song videos from the town's television station, Programme 4. — Read the rest

The post Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Animikii News River] "AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

News River Express

News River Express is back with three articles worth your time, plus a bit of our own news.

We built this summer format so we can spend more time outside with our plants and our people, and still get the best links to your inbox.

This week is the topic everyone is talking about: AI, and where it meets Indigenous Data Sovereignty.

How Indigenous technologists are building data sovereignty into AI

BetaKit

Our CEO and Co-Founder Jeff Ward spoke at Mila's Indigenous AI Gathering in Montréal. The line that stayed with him came from Canada's Minister of AI and Digital Innovation, Evan Solomon:

"AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."

The Minister came to an Indigenous-led space to present and to listen, weeks after Canada's national AI strategy launched. That's where trust starts, and it's how we build at Animikii: technology made with love starts with human hearts and lets love set the pace.

The BetaKit piece covers some of the conversations. Participants in the Indigenous Pathfinders in AI program pitched tools that keep data inside the community network. Diné geneticist Krystal Tsosie put the stakes plainly:

"[Data] determines what the system sees, what it optimizes for, who benefits, and who is harmed. Our community's data is already on this pathway, and the question is whether or not our community governs what it becomes."

Data centers are booming. Indigenous leaders want help protecting their lands.

Grist

AI infrastructure is hungry for land, energy, and water, and Indigenous delegates took that to the UN's Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples. A single hyperscale data centre can draw as much electricity as 100,000 households. Delegates called for free, prior, and informed consent, and some US tribes have passed outright moratoriums. As Sámi Parliament member Maren Storslett said:

"We must not only ask what AI can do, but what it should do."

Respecting First Nations knowledge on Wikimedia platforms

Wikimedia Australia

Wikimedia Australia and the Indigenous-owned firm Terri Janke and Company have released a guide for handling Indigenous knowledge across Wikipedia, Commons, and Wikidata. It matters for AI because Wikimedia trains many large language models, so errors and colonial framing scale with them. The guide uses a risk-assessment approach built on one question: who has the cultural authority to tell this story? As Terri Janke and Company's Shevaun Wright puts it:

"It's not about content moderation, but about content integrity."

And in Animikii news: we are testing ethical AI with an Indigenous community

We've partnered with CEIMIA on a first practical use case for ethical AI and data governance with an Indigenous community in Canada. It pairs two tools: our Niiwin platform, and the Data Roadmap CEIMIA built for the Global Partnership on AI.

Most talk about responsible AI stays at the level of principles. This pilot asks what those principles look like when a community runs them on its own data, with tools it controls. Niiwin carries OCAP®, CARE, and UNDRIP principles into how the system behaves. Testing CEIMIA's roadmap on Indigenous data should push the tool somewhere more inclusive, because community data raises questions government data never does.

It's a small, concrete pilot to help answer a bigger question: can these systems advance Indigenous rights, not just avoid harming them? That's what we're setting out to test. More information on that pilot here.

Want to talk it through? Book an AI conversation and Niiwin demo direct with our CEO Jeff: grab a time.

items.title: [MacRumors] iMac Update Expected This Year, Model With OLED Display Also in Works
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Apple has finished development of a new iMac that it plans to release later this year, according to a report today from Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The updated iMac is one of 11 new Mac models that Apple plans to release over the next two years or so.


The next iMac will unsurprisingly have a newer chip, but the report did not indicate if Apple plans to use the existing M5 chip or the upcoming M6 chip. Apple has never released an iMac with its higher-end "Pro" or "Max" chips.

No major design changes have been rumored for the next iMac, so it will likely continue to have a 24-inch LCD screen. However, the report said Apple is planning a subsequent iMac with an OLED display for release "further in the future."

The current iMac with the M4 chip was announced in October 2024. The desktop computer did not receive an M5 chip upgrade last year, so the model coming this year would represent the first refresh in two years. The report said the iMac will continue to come in two configurations, with varying CPU/GPU core counts and ports.

Apple discontinued the Intel-based iMac Pro and 27-inch iMac models in 2021 and 2022, respectively, and the computer has only come in a 24-inch size since then. Over the years, there have been rumors that an iMac Pro with at least a 30-inch display and Apple silicon might be released one day, but nothing of that sort has materialized.

Apple raised prices on all Macs last month amid rising memory chip costs. In the U.S., the iMac now starts at $1,499, up from $1,299.
Related Roundup: iMac
Tags: Mark Gurman, OLED
Buyer's Guide: iMac (Don't Buy)
Related Forum: iMac

This article, "iMac Update Expected This Year, Model With OLED Display Also in Works" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums
